note_data.title: Data representation
keyPoints: **Floating point** numbers use mantissa and exponent to represent very large and very small decimal numbers
**Normalisation** ensures the mantissa starts with the first significant digit after the decimal point for maximum precision
**Bitmap images** store colour values for each pixel, while **vector images** store mathematical descriptions of shapes
**Sound sampling** converts analogue sound waves to digital by measuring amplitude at regular intervals
**Sample rate** is measured in Hz and determines sound quality - higher sample rate gives better quality but larger file size
**Lossy compression** removes data permanently to reduce file size, **lossless compression** allows perfect reconstruction
**JPEG** is lossy compression for images, **PNG** is lossless compression for images
**MP3** is lossy compression for sound files, reducing quality to achieve smaller file sizes
**Resolution** determines image quality - higher resolution means more pixels and better detail but larger file size

definitions.definition: The fractional part of a floating point number that stores the significant digits
The power part of a floating point number that determines the position of the decimal point
The process of adjusting the mantissa and exponent so the mantissa has its first significant digit immediately after the decimal point
The number of times per second that the amplitude of an analogue sound wave is measured and converted to digital
A compression method that permanently removes some data to reduce file size, resulting in some loss of quality

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give detailed account of the process step by step
Give similarities AND differences between two concepts

modelAnswer.question: Explain why normalisation is used in floating point representation. [3]
modelAnswer.answer: Normalisation ensures the mantissa starts with the first significant digit immediately after the decimal point. This maximises the precision of the number by using all available bits in the mantissa effectively. It also provides a standard format that eliminates multiple representations of the same number.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Mantissa starts with first significant digit after decimal point
Mark 2: Maximises precision/uses all available bits effectively
Mark 3: Provides standard format/eliminates multiple representations

commonMistakes: Never say 'floating point is more accurate' — always say 'floating point can represent a wider range of numbers'
Students often confuse sample rate with bit depth when describing sound quality factors
Never say 'lossy compression loses some data' — always say 'lossy compression permanently removes data that cannot be recovered'
Students mix up mantissa and exponent functions in floating point representation
Don't say 'higher resolution means better quality' without mentioning it also means larger file size
examinerTips: Floating point representation appears in almost every Paper 2 exam
Always show the calculation steps when normalising floating point numbers
For compression questions, always mention both file size AND quality effects
Sound and image representation questions often ask for trade-offs between quality and storage
Draw bit patterns clearly when showing floating point representation
formulas: File size (bits) = width × height × colour depth
Sound file size = sample rate × bit depth × duration × channels
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Floating point representation diagram showing bit allocation between sign bit, exponent bits, and mantissa bits, with example calculation of the decimal value
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to convert the denary number 156 to binary and hexadecimal, then explain why hexadecimal is used by programmers
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P1 Q8 (4 marks) — candidates had to calculate storage requirements for a bitmap image given dimensions and color depth
This topic was examined in M/J 2022 P2 Q2 (5 marks) — students needed to explain the difference between lossy and lossless compression with examples
This topic featured in O/N 2023 P1 Q12 (3 marks) — they tested understanding of ASCII values and character representation in binary

workedExample.question: A sound file is sampled at 44.1 kHz with a bit depth of 16 bits. Calculate the file size for a 3-minute stereo recording. Give your answer in MB. (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: Calculate samples per second = 44,100 Hz
Step 2: Calculate total samples = 44,100 × 180 seconds = 7,938,000 samples
Step 3: Account for stereo (2 channels) = 7,938,000 × 2 = 15,876,000 samples
Step 4: Calculate total bits = 15,876,000 × 16 bits = 254,016,000 bits
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct calculation of total samples, 1 mark for accounting for stereo channels, 1 mark for applying bit depth correctly, 1 mark for final answer with correct units

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused bits and bytes in storage calculations' — always clearly state whether your answer is in bits or bytes and convert appropriately
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students often forgot that stereo files have two channels' — always check if audio questions mention mono or stereo recording
Examiner Report 2023: 'Candidates frequently gave incorrect examples of file formats for compression types' — remember JPEG is lossy, PNG is lossless, not the other way around
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw a number base conversion table showing denary (0-15), binary (0000-1111), and hexadecimal (0-F) equivalents. Include column headers and show the relationship between 4 binary digits equaling 1 hexadecimal digit. Also draw a simple bitmap representation showing how pixels are stored as binary values with different bit depths (1-bit, 8-bit, 24-bit examples).
note_data.quickRecap: Data is represented in binary (0s and 1s) in computer systems. Remember: 8 bits = 1 byte, hexadecimal uses base 16 (0-F), and file sizes depend on sampling rate × bit depth × duration × channels for audio. ASCII represents characters as 7-bit codes. Compression reduces file sizes - lossy (JPEG) removes data permanently, lossless (PNG) allows perfect reconstruction.
